title: Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis

patient.name: Tyler Jackson
patient.persona: active middle school student, worried about missing basketball season

chief_complaint: My son's face and legs are swollen. His urine looks like Coca-Cola. He had a sore throat about 2 weeks ago that got better on its own.

physical_exam.General: Alert boy, periorbital edema, mild facial puffiness
physical_exam.HEENT: Periorbital edema, no pharyngeal erythema or exudates
physical_exam.Cardiac: Regular rhythm, no murmurs. No pericardial rub.
physical_exam.Abdomen: Mild ascites, no masses, no tenderness
physical_exam.Extremities: Pitting edema to mid-shins bilaterally. No arthritis. No purpura.
physical_exam.Skin: No rash, no petechiae, no purpura

hidden_data.urinalysis: Cola-colored (gross hematuria). Protein 3+. Specific gravity 1.025. RBCs: Too numerous to count (>100/hpf). RBC casts present. Dysmorphic RBCs (acanthocytes). WBCs: 5-10/hpf. No bacteria.
hidden_data.urine_protein_creatinine: UPCR 2.8 g/g (nephrotic-range proteinuria in a child)
hidden_data.cmp: Sodium 140, Potassium 4.8, Chloride 102, HCO3 22, BUN 28, Creatinine 1.4 (baseline 0.6), Glucose 90
hidden_data.aso_titer: Anti-Streptolysin O (ASO) titer 800 IU/mL (strongly positive - normal <200)
hidden_data.throat_culture: Group A Streptococcus (GAS) positive (even though pharynx appears normal now)
hidden_data.renal_us: Both kidneys normal size (Right 11.5cm, Left 11.8cm). Increased cortical echogenicity. Normal corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis.
hidden_data.cxr: Small bilateral pleural effusions. Heart size normal. No pulmonary edema.
hidden_data.ecg: Sinus rhythm. No LVH. No pericarditis changes.

key_learning_points: APSGN = immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis occurring 1-3 weeks after Group A Streptococcus infection (pharyngitis) or 3-6 weeks after skin infection (impetigo)
Classic triad: Hematuria (cola-colored urine) + Edema + Hypertension in a child post-streptococcal infection
Low C3 with normal C4 = activation of alternative complement pathway (classic for APSGN). C3 normalizes in 6-8 weeks
ASO titer is positive in 80% of pharyngeal-associated APSGN. Anti-DNase B more sensitive for skin infections
Treatment is SUPPORTIVE: Sodium/fluid restriction, diuretics, antihypertensives. NO steroids or immunosuppression needed. >95% recover completely.

patient_responses.mother: He had a really bad sore throat 2 weeks ago with fever. It went away on its own in 4-5 days so I didn't take him to the doctor. Now his face is puffy and his ankles are swollen.
patient_responses.tyler: I feel okay just tired. My face looks weird. I noticed my pee was dark like Coke yesterday. I have basketball tryouts next week - I need to be better!
patient_responses.medications: No regular medications. No allergies to medications.
patient_responses.past: Never been hospitalized. No known kidney problems before. All vaccines up to date.

hints.history: Hematuria + edema + HTN in a child 1-3 weeks after sore throat is classic for APSGN. Ask about skin infections (impetigo) which have longer latency (3-6 weeks).
hints.examination: Periorbital and peripheral edema. Hypertension is common. Look for signs of fluid overload (pulmonary edema). Examine for pharyngitis, impetigo, and purpura.
hints.labs: Urinalysis: RBC casts are pathognomonic for glomerulonephritis. Dysmorphic RBCs indicate glomerular source. ASO titer, complement (low C3, normal C4), renal function.
hints.imaging: Renal ultrasound shows normal/large kidneys (acute process, not CKD). CXR for pulmonary edema/effusions from fluid overload.

feedback.correct_message: Excellent diagnosis! You correctly identified APSGN from the classic presentation: gross hematuria (cola-colored urine) + edema + hypertension in a child 2 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis. The low C3 (with normal C4), elevated ASO, and RBC casts confirm the diagnosis. Management is supportive with sodium restriction, diuretics, and BP control. Prognosis is excellent.
feedback.partially_correct_message: Good recognition of glomerulonephritis! The low C3 with normal C4 and elevated ASO specifically point to post-streptococcal GN, not IgA nephropathy (normal complements, often concurrent with URI) or lupus nephritis (low C3 AND C4, positive ANA). The treatment is supportive - no steroids needed for APSGN.
feedback.incorrect_message: The classic triad (cola-colored urine, edema, HTN) in a child 2 weeks after strep throat with RBC casts, low C3 (normal C4), and elevated ASO is textbook APSGN. This is NOT nephrotic syndrome alone (has hematuria and HTN). It is NOT UTI (RBC casts, no bacteria, no WBCs). Treatment is supportive - diuretics, BP control, fluid/sodium restriction. Do NOT give steroids.
clinical_pearls: RBC casts are virtually pathognomonic for glomerulonephritis - they indicate bleeding from within the glomerulus
Low C3 with normal C4 = alternative complement pathway activation (APSGN). Low C3 + low C4 = classical pathway (lupus, cryoglobulinemia)
ASO peaks at 3-5 weeks post-infection. Early testing (within first week) may be falsely negative

urinalysis.comment: RBC casts are pathognomonic for GN. Dysmorphic RBCs indicate glomerular source. 3+ proteinuria but not pure nephrotic

complement.comment: Low C3 (18) with normal C4 (25) = alternative pathway activation. Classic for APSGN. Will normalize in 6-8 weeks

aso_titer.comment: ASO 800 = recent streptococcal infection confirmed. Supports diagnosis

cmp.comment: Creatinine 1.4 from baseline 0.6 = AKI from acute GN. BUN 28 from volume depletion and decreased GFR

missing_tests: Anti-DNase B - More sensitive for skin-related APSGN. ANA, ANCA if suspecting other GN causes
feedback.attending_note: Doctor, this is classic APSGN. Sodium restriction <2g/day. Fluid restriction 1L + urine output/day. Furosemide 1-2mg/kg IV for diuresis (monitor K+). Amlodipine 2.5mg PO for HTN (target BP <95th percentile for age). No steroids, no immunosuppression - they are not indicated and may worsen outcome. Counsel that gross hematuria resolves in 1-2 weeks, microscopic hematuria may persist 6-12 months, proteinuria resolves in 3-6 months. >95% complete recovery in children. Follow nephrology and check C3, creatinine, and urinalysis in 6-8 weeks.
